summary: Five Davidic laments of trust: the soul under siege finds God alone as refuge, as David cries out from Gath, from exile among hostile peoples, and from the cave — each psalm a movement from desperate petition to confident praise.
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
56Lament / TrustDavidSeized by the Philistines in Gath (1 Sam 21:10–15)Fearless trust in God when surrounded by mortal enemies"In God I trust and am not afraid. What can man do to me?" (v.4, 11)Christ delivered up to enemies yet trusting the Father; Heb 2:13 quotes the trust-formula
120LamentUnknown (Song of Ascents)Living among deceitful, warlike people far from JerusalemLonging for peace while dwelling in a hostile world"I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war." (v.7)Believers as strangers and aliens (1 Pet 2:11) longing for the heavenly city
140Lament / ImprecatoryDavidThreatened by violent, scheming enemiesRescue from the violent; God as defender of the afflicted"I know that the LORD secures justice for the poor and upholds the cause of the needy." (v.12)Christ the righteous sufferer; God vindicating the oppressed through the cross
141Lament / PetitionDavidDanger; temptation to join the wicked; need for integrityPrayer for a guarded mouth and perseverance in righteousness"May my prayer be set before you like incense; the lifting up of my hands like the evening sacrifice." (v.2)Rev 8:3–4 (incense as prayers of the saints); Christ interceding as our great High Priest
142LamentDavid (Maskil)Hiding in the cave, utterly alone (1 Sam 22 or 24)God as sole refuge when no human help can be found"When my spirit grows faint within me, it is you who watch over my way." (v.3)Christ forsaken on the cross yet crying to the Father; the resurrection as God's ultimate deliverance
